perturbation.original_text: Provider shall submit or cause to be submitted to Recipient in writing, within 15 days after the end of each month, an invoice setting forth the Fees for the Services provided to Recipient during such month in reasonable detail, as applicable, due under such invoice.
perturbation.changed_text: Provider shall submit or cause to be submitted to Recipient in writing, within 45 days after the end of each month, an invoice setting forth the Fees for the Services provided to Recipient during such month in reasonable detail, as applicable, due under such invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the invoice submission timeline to 45 days could violate prompt payment laws in some states.
perturbation.law_explanation: In Illinois, the Prompt Payment Act for business and commerce transactions (815 ILCS 505/2) requires payments to be made, in general, according to the terms of the contract. If the contract is silent, it requires payment within 30 days of receipt of invoice and approval of the work. By specifying 45 days, the contract introduces the risk of conflicting with the statutory 30-day default, especially if the goods and services are approved faster, thus potentially violating the Illinois Prompt Payment Act.

Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 815 ILCS 505/2 (815 ILCS 505/2) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 262) Sec. 2. Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices, including but not limited to the use or employment of any deception fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation or the concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact, with intent that others rely upon the concealment, suppression or omission of such material fact, or the use or employment of any practice described in Section 2 of the "Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act", approved August 5, 1965, in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful whether any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby. In construing this section consideration shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to Section 5 (a) of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (Source: P.A. 78-904 .) perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the invoice submission timeline to 45 days, while the Law Explanation suggests this may conflict with Illinois' Prompt Payment Act, which mandates payment within 30 days if the contract is silent. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates potential violations of prompt payment laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references unfair practices and deceptive acts, which could arguably include extending payment terms beyond a reasonable timeframe implied by prompt payment standards. The changed text potentially contradicts legal prompt payment standards, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall pay, or cause to be paid, the Fees shown on an invoice no later than the last business day of the month Recipient received such invoice unless disputed in accordance with Section 5.7.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall pay, or cause to be paid, the Fees shown on an invoice no later than 60 days after Recipient received such invoice unless disputed in accordance with Section 5.7.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment deadline to 60 days may violate prompt payment laws in certain states, requiring faster payment timelines.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Prompt Payment Act (815 ILCS 505/2) typically requires payments to be made within 30 days of receipt of an invoice and approval of the services/goods, unless otherwise specified in a contract. This change sets a 60-day payment term, contradicting the 30-day requirement of the Illinois Prompt Payment Act when no different term is negotiated.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment deadline to 60 days, while the Law Explanation states that the Illinois Prompt Payment Act typically requires payments within 30 days, creating a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 815 ILCS 505/2, discusses unfair practices, indicating a potential violation with the extended payment term. This falls under 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligation (payment deadline) violates state law (Prompt Payment Act), making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Company may terminate for convenience any Transition Service, and RGHI may terminate for convenience any Reverse Transition Service, upon 30 days' prior written notice of such termination
perturbation.changed_text: Company may terminate for convenience any Transition Service, and RGHI may terminate for convenience any Reverse Transition Service, upon 14 days' prior written notice of such termination
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice to 14 days could be problematic in states requiring longer notice periods for service contract terminations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The federal Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act (WARN) Act, 29 U.S. Code § 2102, requires 60 calendar days advance notification of plant closings and mass layoffs. This applies to employers with 100 or more employees. If the services being terminated result in layoffs of a significant number of employees the 14 days' notice would contradict the WARN Act.

Notice required before plant closings and mass layoffs (a) Notice to employees, State dislocated worker units, and local governments An employer shall not order a plant closing or mass layoff until the end of a 60-day period after the employer serves written notice of such an order- (1) to each representative of the affected employees as of the time of the notice or, if there is no such representative a
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 14 days. The Law Explanation points to the WARN Act (29 U.S. Code § 2102), which requires 60 days' notice for plant closings and mass layoffs. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 29 USC 2102, confirms this 60-day requirement. If the termination of service results in mass layoffs, the 14-day notice in the modified contract directly contradicts the WARN Act's 60-day notice requirement, making the contract potentially non-compliant and therefore creating a legal contradiction under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.
